summary: The post highlights a Harvard research paper that experimentally studied Chinese social media censorship by creating accounts, submitting test posts, and observing which were removed. The researchers even contracted with Chinese firms to install censoring technology on their own site, allowing them to reverse-engineer how it works. Their key finding was that criticism of government leaders is allowed, but posts about events that could spark collective action are censored. O'Neil notes the irony of getting help from Chinese firms to study censorship while acknowledging a sociologist's caveat that local officials' personal interests in maintaining stability may matter more than coherent central policy.
key_points: Researchers conducted the first large-scale experimental study of Chinese censorship by creating social media accounts and randomly submitting different texts
They reverse-engineered censorship technology by contracting with Chinese firms to install the same systems on their own site
The study found that criticism of the state is permitted, but posts with 'collective action potential' are censored
A caveat notes that local officials' personal interests in suppressing social movements may be more important than central government policy
money_quotes: Criticisms of the state, its leaders, and their policies are published, whereas posts about real-world events with collective action potential are censored
Interesting that they got so much help from the Chinese to censor their posts
I'm a sucker for reverse-engineering powerful algorithms, even when there are major caveats
Local officials have a personal interest in suppressing content that could lead to social movements
key_insight: Chinese censorship prioritizes preventing collective action over suppressing criticism of the government itself.
